message.content:
Let's brainstorm this. I want to use Lama indexes light parser, which is an open-source LLM tool that can parse documents, PDFs, images using OCR, and hopefully an HTML page, which allows me to create an LLM knowledge base.

Here is what we are going to do. When the user comes, the user is presented with a front-end web page, which will be built on Vue.js with Tailwind Plus, since I already have a subscription to Tailwind Plus. We will use that to create a front-end Vue.js page which invites the user into first creating an application on the platform. Once they create an application, then  They can add an organization, and once they create an organization in the application, then inside the organization they have the ability to create the knowledge graph for it. The idea is that we will use light parser to scrape through websites, and then we will allow the user to input multiple stuff for the same particular organization. Let's say the application that is created as a voice agent and the organization is an educational institute called MIT, Massachusetts Institute of Technology. Inside MIT, the user, which will probably be an admin user, can go ahead and add a URL, a website URL of MIT, which points to multiple courses, the college life, and what not. The user also has the ability to upload images, PDF docs, markdown files, you name it.
We will use light parser by Lama index to scrape through all of it, then use an LLM model behind the scenes to create a knowledge graph out of it and hopefully a vector embedding for it. We will save this vector embedding inside a parade DB with a PG vector extension. Once the embedding is created, then the user has again three to four options on how to interact with the particular data or the knowledge graph:
1. A chatbot inside a chatbot that can be embedded in any of the user's websites. This chatbot can then communicate with the knowledge base using any LLM tools.
2. The user also has the option to create a voice agent, which will just be a voice interface on top of the knowledge base. We will use a WAD or some speech-to-text tools like Open Whispering, in which the user can talk to the particular knowledge graph and get information out of it.
3. We provide a web RTC extension in which they can connect to it on their WhatsApp calls.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/jobinlawrance/.claude/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec review loop** — dispatch spec-document-reviewer subagent with precisely crafted review context (never your session history); fix issues and re-dispatch until approved (max 3 iterations, then surface to human)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec review loop" [shape=box];
    "Spec review passed?" [shape=diamond];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec review loop";
    "Spec review loop" -> "Spec review passed?";
    "Spec review passed?" -> "Spec review loop" [label="issues found,\nfix and re-dispatch"];
    "Spec review passed?" -> "User reviews spec?" [label="approved"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Review Loop:**
After writing the spec document:

1. Dispatch spec-document-reviewer subagent (see spec-document-reviewer-prompt.md)
2. If Issues Found: fix, re-dispatch, repeat until Approved
3. If loop exceeds 3 iterations, surface to human for guidance

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

## LlamaParse Research Findings

### 1. Supported Document Types

LlamaParse supports **90+ file formats** including:
- **PDF** (its primary strength -- complex layouts, scanned docs, multi-column)
- **Office documents**: .docx, .pptx, .xlsx
- **HTML**
- **Images** (multimodal parsing -- charts, tables embedded in images)
- **Other unstructured formats** broadly

Key capabilities: table recognition, layout-aware parsing (headers/footers/split sections), multimodal parsing (charts, images, visual elements), OCR with 100+ language support, and handwriting/checkbox recognition.

It does **not** do web scraping or URL crawling -- see point 3.

### 2. Python Package Name and Version

There is an important **package migration** happening:

- **Legacy package**: `llama-parse` (v0.6.94, last release Feb 13, 2026) -- **deprecated**, maintained only until May 1, 2026
- **Legacy package**: `llama-cloud-services` (v0.6.94) -- also deprecated
- **New canonical package**: `llama-cloud` (v1.6.0, released Mar 5, 2026) -- this is the current recommended package. Install via `pip install llama-cloud>=1.0`. Repo: `github.com/run-llama/llama-cloud-py`

### 3. Web Scraping / URL Crawling

**LlamaParse has no web scraping or URL crawling capability.** It is strictly a document parser -- you upload files to it. For web content, you would need a separate tool (e.g., Firecrawl, Crawl4AI, Playwright, or similar). LlamaCloud platform does offer data source connectors (S3, Azure Blob, Google Drive, OneDrive, SharePoint, Box, Confluence) for ingesting files from cloud storage, but these are not web crawlers.

### 4. Integration with Knowledge Graphs and Vector Embeddings

LlamaParse itself outputs clean markdown/text/structured data. The integration path is:

- **Vector embeddings**: LlamaParse output feeds directly into LlamaIndex's indexing pipeline. You parse documents, then create a `VectorStoreIndex` from the parsed nodes. LlamaCloud platform also offers managed indexes with built-in embedding and retrieval.
- **Knowledge graphs**: LlamaIndex OSS has a `PropertyGraphIndex` and `PropertyGraphStore` (supports Neo4j and others). You parse documents with LlamaParse, then use LlamaIndex's graph extractors (e.g., `SchemaLLMPathExtractor`, `ImplicitPathExtractor`) to build knowledge graphs from the parsed text. This is a two-step process: parse then extract -- LlamaParse does not directly produce knowledge graph triples.

### 5. Licensing Terms

**LlamaParse is NOT open source in the traditional sense. It is a freemium cloud service:**

- The **Python client SDK** (`llama-cloud`) is MIT-licensed open source
- The **parsing engine itself** runs as a proprietary cloud API hosted by LlamaIndex
- **Pricing tiers**:
  - **Free**: 10K credits, 1 user, 1 project
  - **Starter**: 40K credits included, pay-as-you-go up to 400K ($500), 5 users
  - **Pro**: 400K credits included, pay-as-you-go up to 4M ($5K), 10 users
  - **Enterprise**: custom volume, hybrid cloud deployment, SSO, unlimited users
- Credit cost: **1,000 credits = $1.25**
- There is no self-hosted open-source version of the parsing engine. Enterprise customers can get "hybrid cloud deployment" but that is a paid arrangement.

### Key Takeaway for Our Use Case

LlamaParse is excellent for document parsing (PDF, DOCX, PPTX, etc.) but we would need **separate tools** for: (a) web scraping/crawling, and (b) knowledge graph extraction (though LlamaIndex OSS provides the graph extraction pipeline that can consume LlamaParse output). The service is freemium with a generous free tier but the core parsing engine is proprietary.

content.text:
I now have enough information from all the sources. Here is the research summary.

---

## LlamaParse Research Findings

### 1. Supported Document Types

LlamaParse supports **90+ file formats** including:
- **PDF** (its primary strength -- complex layouts, scanned docs, multi-column)
- **Office documents**: .docx, .pptx, .xlsx
- **HTML**
- **Images** (multimodal parsing -- charts, tables embedded in images)
- **Other unstructured formats** broadly

Key capabilities: table recognition, layout-aware parsing (headers/footers/split sections), multimodal parsing (charts, images, visual elements), OCR with 100+ language support, and handwriting/checkbox recognition.

It does **not** do web scraping or URL crawling -- see point 3.

### 2. Python Package Name and Version

There is an important **package migration** happening:

- **Legacy package**: `llama-parse` (v0.6.94, last release Feb 13, 2026) -- **deprecated**, maintained only until May 1, 2026
- **Legacy package**: `llama-cloud-services` (v0.6.94) -- also deprecated
- **New canonical package**: `llama-cloud` (v1.6.0, released Mar 5, 2026) -- this is the current recommended package. Install via `pip install llama-cloud>=1.0`. Repo: `github.com/run-llama/llama-cloud-py`

### 3. Web Scraping / URL Crawling

**LlamaParse has no web scraping or URL crawling capability.** It is strictly a document parser -- you upload files to it. For web content, you would need a separate tool (e.g., Firecrawl, Crawl4AI, Playwright, or similar). LlamaCloud platform does offer data source connectors (S3, Azure Blob, Google Drive, OneDrive, SharePoint, Box, Confluence) for ingesting files from cloud storage, but these are not web crawlers.

### 4. Integration with Knowledge Graphs and Vector Embeddings

LlamaParse itself outputs clean markdown/text/structured data. The integration path is:

- **Vector embeddings**: LlamaParse output feeds directly into LlamaIndex's indexing pipeline. You parse documents, then create a `VectorStoreIndex` from the parsed nodes. LlamaCloud platform also offers managed indexes with built-in embedding and retrieval.
- **Knowledge graphs**: LlamaIndex OSS has a `PropertyGraphIndex` and `PropertyGraphStore` (supports Neo4j and others). You parse documents with LlamaParse, then use LlamaIndex's graph extractors (e.g., `SchemaLLMPathExtractor`, `ImplicitPathExtractor`) to build knowledge graphs from the parsed text. This is a two-step process: parse then extract -- LlamaParse does not directly produce knowledge graph triples.

### 5. Licensing Terms

**LlamaParse is NOT open source in the traditional sense. It is a freemium cloud service:**

- The **Python client SDK** (`llama-cloud`) is MIT-licensed open source
- The **parsing engine itself** runs as a proprietary cloud API hosted by LlamaIndex
- **Pricing tiers**:
  - **Free**: 10K credits, 1 user, 1 project
  - **Starter**: 40K credits included, pay-as-you-go up to 400K ($500), 5 users
  - **Pro**: 400K credits included, pay-as-you-go up to 4M ($5K), 10 users
  - **Enterprise**: custom volume, hybrid cloud deployment, SSO, unlimited users
- Credit cost: **1,000 credits = $1.25**
- There is no self-hosted open-source version of the parsing engine. Enterprise customers can get "hybrid cloud deployment" but that is a paid arrangement.

### Key Takeaway for Our Use Case

LlamaParse is excellent for document parsing (PDF, DOCX, PPTX, etc.) but we would need **separate tools** for: (a) web scraping/crawling, and (b) knowledge graph extraction (though LlamaIndex OSS provides the graph extraction pipeline that can consume LlamaParse output). The service is freemium with a generous free tier but the core parsing engine is proprietary.

**Actions taken:**
- Ran 9 web searches covering STT, TTS, VAD, WebRTC, WhatsApp integration, and voice agent frameworks
- Synthesized findings into a structured research document

- `/Users/jobinlawrance/Project/raven/docs/voice-agent-webrtc-research.md` -- full research document with comparison tables, architecture diagrams, and source links

**Key findings:**

1. **STT**: faster-whisper and Deepgram Nova-3 are the best real-time options. NVIDIA Parakeet TDT (RTFx ~2000) is the fastest self-hosted model. Whisper.cpp for CPU/edge.

2. **WebRTC**: LiveKit (open-source Go SFU) and Daily.co are the two dominant WebRTC platforms for voice agents. Both have mature agent SDKs.

3. **TTS**: Piper (MIT, runs on Raspberry Pi) for lightweight self-hosted. Coqui XTTS v2.5 for voice cloning (company shut down but code lives on via idiap fork). Cartesia Sonic for lowest-latency API.

4. **VAD**: Silero VAD is the industry standard (MIT, <1ms per chunk, used by LiveKit and Pipecat). @ricky0123/vad wraps it for browser JS. TEN VAD is a strong emerging alternative.

5. **WhatsApp**: **Proven and production-ready.** Meta launched the WhatsApp Business Calling API (July 2025) using WebRTC for media and Graph API webhooks for SDP signaling. Your server receives an SDP offer via webhook, generates an SDP answer, and establishes a bidirectional WebRTC audio channel. No unofficial bridges needed.

6. **Frameworks**: **LiveKit Agents** (best for WebRTC-first, low-latency) and **Pipecat** (most flexible, transport-agnostic, 20+ provider integrations) are the two recommended frameworks. TEN Framework offers graph-based architecture for advanced use cases. All are open source. Typical round-trip latency is 500-800ms.

2. **WebRTC**: LiveKit (open-source Go SFU) and Daily.co are the two dominant WebRTC platforms for voice agents. Both have mature agent SDKs.

Your server receives an SDP offer via webhook, generates an SDP answer, and establishes a bidirectional WebRTC audio channel. No unofficial bridges needed.

Research complete. Here is the summary:

**Actions taken:**
- Fetched and indexed 10 ParadeDB documentation pages (docs home, architecture, guarantees, limitations, roadmap, deploy overview, install guide, quickstart, AI integration, website)
- Fetched and indexed the ParadeDB GitHub README
- Synthesized findings into a structured research document

**File created:**
- `/Users/jobinlawrance/Project/raven/docs/research/paradedb-pgvector-research.md` -- comprehensive research document covering all 5 questions

**Key findings:**

1. **ParadeDB is a full-text search extension, not a vector database.** It adds Elastic-quality BM25 search to Postgres via `pg_search`. Vector search and hybrid search are on the roadmap but not yet shipping (v0.22.3).

2. **Deployment options:** Docker image, Kubernetes Helm chart (CloudNativePG), extension install on existing Postgres, one-click deploys to Railway/Render/DigitalOcean, and Enterprise BYOC on AWS/GCP. A fully managed cloud is in progress.

3. **Vector similarity search requires pgvector alongside ParadeDB.** They are complementary extensions on the same Postgres instance. Native hybrid search (BM25 + vector in one query) is planned but currently requires manual Reciprocal Rank Fusion.

4. **Performance:** LSM tree architecture with inverted + columnar indexes, real-time indexing within transactions, MVCC-compliant concurrency, production deployments up to 10TB. Citus-compatible for distributed workloads.

5. **Critical caveat:** ParadeDB Community lacks WAL support -- **Enterprise license is required for production durability**. Without WALs, data loss is possible on crash.

6. **For a knowledge base:** The pgvector + ParadeDB combination on a single Postgres is architecturally compelling (no ETL, single operational target), but today you must manually fuse BM25 and vector results. Purpose-built vector DBs (Pinecone, Qdrant, Weaviate) offer turnkey hybrid search and better horizontal scaling if that is the primary workload.

content.text:
Here is a comprehensive research summary of LiteParse.

---

## 1. What is LiteParse? How does it differ from LlamaParse?

**LiteParse** is an open-source, standalone document parsing tool by LlamaIndex (run-llama) focused on **fast, lightweight, local text extraction** with spatial layout information and bounding boxes. It is written in TypeScript and published as an npm package (`@llamaindex/liteparse`, currently v1.3.1).

**Key differences from LlamaParse:**

| | LiteParse | LlamaParse |
|---|---|---|
| **Runs where** | Entirely local, on your machine | Cloud-based (cloud.llamaindex.ai) |
| **LLM usage** | None -- no LLM, no API keys | Uses proprietary LLM features for document understanding |
| **Cost** | Free, Apache 2.0 | Freemium cloud service |
| **Strengths** | Fast text extraction, spatial layout, bounding boxes | Dense tables, multi-column layouts, charts, handwritten text, scanned PDFs |
| **Output** | Text or JSON with bounding boxes | Clean structured data and markdown |

LlamaIndex positions LiteParse as the "fast and light" local option, and LlamaParse as the "heavy-duty" cloud option for complex documents. The README explicitly says: "For complex documents (dense tables, multi-column layouts, charts, handwritten text, or scanned PDFs), you'll get significantly better results with LlamaParse."

---

## 2. Is it truly open-source and self-hostable?

**Yes.** It is fully open-source and self-hostable:

- **License:** Apache 2.0
- **No cloud dependency:** The docs state "It runs entirely on your machine, with no cloud dependencies, no LLMs, no API keys."
- **No phone-home:** Everything runs locally. The only network call is Tesseract.js downloading language model data on first use, and even that can be pre-cached for air-gapped environments via the `TESSDATA_PREFIX` env variable.
- **Self-contained binary:** Works as a global npm CLI tool or a Homebrew package on macOS/Linux.

---

## 3. What document types does it support?

LiteParse natively parses PDFs and supports additional formats via automatic conversion:

**Natively supported:**
- **PDF** -- parsed directly using PDF.js with spatial text extraction

**Via LibreOffice (must be installed separately):**
- Word: `.doc`, `.docx`, `.docm`, `.odt`, `.rtf`
- PowerPoint: `.ppt`, `.pptx`, `.pptm`, `.odp`
- Spreadsheets: `.xls`, `.xlsx`, `.xlsm`, `.ods`, `.csv`, `.tsv`

**Via ImageMagick (must be installed separately):**
- Images: `.jpg`, `.jpeg`, `.png`, `.gif`, `.bmp`, `.tiff`, `.webp`, `.svg`
- Images are converted to PDF then parsed with OCR to extract text.

**OCR for scanned content:**
- Built-in Tesseract.js (zero-setup, works out of the box)
- Or plug in any HTTP OCR server (EasyOCR, PaddleOCR, or custom) via a simple API spec

**Not supported:**
- HTML parsing / web page scraping
- Markdown input parsing

The conversion pipeline is: detect file extension -> convert to PDF via LibreOffice or ImageMagick if needed -> parse the PDF.

---

## 4. Can it scrape/parse web pages given a URL?

**No, it cannot scrape or parse web pages.** There is no HTML-to-text or web scraping capability.

What it *can* do with URLs is parse **remote document files** (PDFs, etc.) by downloading them first:

- **CLI:** Pipe a downloaded file via stdin: `curl -sL https://example.com/report.pdf | lit parse -`
- **Library:** Fetch the bytes yourself and pass a `Buffer`/`Uint8Array` to the parser:
  ```typescript
  const response = await fetch("https://example.com/report.pdf");
  const buffer = Buffer.from(await response.arrayBuffer());
  const result = await parser.parse(buffer);
  ```

This only works for actual document files (PDF, DOCX, images, etc.) hosted at a URL -- not for parsing the HTML content of a web page.

---

## 5. What are its dependencies and how is it installed?

**Installation options:**
- `npm i -g @llamaindex/liteparse` (recommended)
- `brew tap run-llama/liteparse && brew install llamaindex-liteparse` (macOS/Linux)
- Install from source via git clone + build
- As a library: `npm install @llamaindex/liteparse`
- Python wrapper: `pip install liteparse` (calls the Node CLI under the hood)

**Runtime requirements:**
- Node.js >= 18.0.0

**Core npm dependencies (from package.json):**
- `@hyzyla/pdfium` -- PDF rendering engine
- `sharp` -- image processing
- `tesseract.js` v7 -- built-in OCR
- `axios` -- HTTP client (for OCR server communication)
- `commander` -- CLI framework
- `file-type` -- file format detection
- `form-data` -- multipart form handling
- `p-limit` -- concurrency control
- `unified` -- content processing
- `zod` -- schema validation

**Optional system dependencies (for non-PDF formats):**
- **LibreOffice** -- required only for Office documents (doc, docx, xlsx, pptx, etc.)
- **ImageMagick** -- required only for image files (png, jpg, tiff, etc.)

---

## 6. How does it output parsed content?

LiteParse outputs in two formats, selected via `--format`:

### Text format (default: `--format text`)
Plain text with spatial layout preserved. Text comes back positioned as it appears on the page. This is the stdout default.

### JSON format (`--format json`)
Structured JSON containing per-page data with precise bounding boxes for every text item:
```typescript
// Each text item includes:
{
  text: string,
  x: number,      // position
  y: number,
  width: number,
  height: number
}
```

The library API returns a result object with:
- `result.text` -- full document text with layout preserved
- `result.pages` -- array of per-page data, each with `textItems` array
- `result.json?.pages` -- structured JSON with bounding box coordinates per text item

**LiteParse does NOT output markdown.** Its output is either plain text or structured JSON with spatial/bounding-box data. This is a notable difference from LlamaParse, which outputs clean markdown. If you need markdown output, you would need to post-process the text/JSON yourself or use LlamaParse instead.

Additionally, the `lit screenshot` command can generate **PNG or JPG page images** at configurable DPI, useful for feeding pages to LLMs as visual input.

content.text:
Here is a comprehensive research summary of LiteParse.

---

## 1. What is LiteParse? How does it differ from LlamaParse?

**LiteParse** is an open-source, standalone document parsing tool by LlamaIndex (run-llama) focused on **fast, lightweight, local text extraction** with spatial layout information and bounding boxes. It is written in TypeScript and published as an npm package (`@llamaindex/liteparse`, currently v1.3.1).

**Key differences from LlamaParse:**

| | LiteParse | LlamaParse |
|---|---|---|
| **Runs where** | Entirely local, on your machine | Cloud-based (cloud.llamaindex.ai) |
| **LLM usage** | None -- no LLM, no API keys | Uses proprietary LLM features for document understanding |
| **Cost** | Free, Apache 2.0 | Freemium cloud service |
| **Strengths** | Fast text extraction, spatial layout, bounding boxes | Dense tables, multi-column layouts, charts, handwritten text, scanned PDFs |
| **Output** | Text or JSON with bounding boxes | Clean structured data and markdown |

LlamaIndex positions LiteParse as the "fast and light" local option, and LlamaParse as the "heavy-duty" cloud option for complex documents. The README explicitly says: "For complex documents (dense tables, multi-column layouts, charts, handwritten text, or scanned PDFs), you'll get significantly better results with LlamaParse."

---

## 2. Is it truly open-source and self-hostable?

**Yes.** It is fully open-source and self-hostable:

- **License:** Apache 2.0
- **No cloud dependency:** The docs state "It runs entirely on your machine, with no cloud dependencies, no LLMs, no API keys."
- **No phone-home:** Everything runs locally. The only network call is Tesseract.js downloading language model data on first use, and even that can be pre-cached for air-gapped environments via the `TESSDATA_PREFIX` env variable.
- **Self-contained binary:** Works as a global npm CLI tool or a Homebrew package on macOS/Linux.

---

## 3. What document types does it support?

LiteParse natively parses PDFs and supports additional formats via automatic conversion:

**Natively supported:**
- **PDF** -- parsed directly using PDF.js with spatial text extraction

**Via LibreOffice (must be installed separately):**
- Word: `.doc`, `.docx`, `.docm`, `.odt`, `.rtf`
- PowerPoint: `.ppt`, `.pptx`, `.pptm`, `.odp`
- Spreadsheets: `.xls`, `.xlsx`, `.xlsm`, `.ods`, `.csv`, `.tsv`

**Via ImageMagick (must be installed separately):**
- Images: `.jpg`, `.jpeg`, `.png`, `.gif`, `.bmp`, `.tiff`, `.webp`, `.svg`
- Images are converted to PDF then parsed with OCR to extract text.

**OCR for scanned content:**
- Built-in Tesseract.js (zero-setup, works out of the box)
- Or plug in any HTTP OCR server (EasyOCR, PaddleOCR, or custom) via a simple API spec

**Not supported:**
- HTML parsing / web page scraping
- Markdown input parsing

The conversion pipeline is: detect file extension -> convert to PDF via LibreOffice or ImageMagick if needed -> parse the PDF.

---

## 4. Can it scrape/parse web pages given a URL?

**No, it cannot scrape or parse web pages.** There is no HTML-to-text or web scraping capability.

What it *can* do with URLs is parse **remote document files** (PDFs, etc.) by downloading them first:

- **CLI:** Pipe a downloaded file via stdin: `curl -sL https://example.com/report.pdf | lit parse -`
- **Library:** Fetch the bytes yourself and pass a `Buffer`/`Uint8Array` to the parser:
  ```typescript
  const response = await fetch("https://example.com/report.pdf");
  const buffer = Buffer.from(await response.arrayBuffer());
  const result = await parser.parse(buffer);
  ```

This only works for actual document files (PDF, DOCX, images, etc.) hosted at a URL -- not for parsing the HTML content of a web page.

---

## 5. What are its dependencies and how is it installed?

**Installation options:**
- `npm i -g @llamaindex/liteparse` (recommended)
- `brew tap run-llama/liteparse && brew install llamaindex-liteparse` (macOS/Linux)
- Install from source via git clone + build
- As a library: `npm install @llamaindex/liteparse`
- Python wrapper: `pip install liteparse` (calls the Node CLI under the hood)

**Runtime requirements:**
- Node.js >= 18.0.0

**Core npm dependencies (from package.json):**
- `@hyzyla/pdfium` -- PDF rendering engine
- `sharp` -- image processing
- `tesseract.js` v7 -- built-in OCR
- `axios` -- HTTP client (for OCR server communication)
- `commander` -- CLI framework
- `file-type` -- file format detection
- `form-data` -- multipart form handling
- `p-limit` -- concurrency control
- `unified` -- content processing
- `zod` -- schema validation

**Optional system dependencies (for non-PDF formats):**
- **LibreOffice** -- required only for Office documents (doc, docx, xlsx, pptx, etc.)
- **ImageMagick** -- required only for image files (png, jpg, tiff, etc.)

---

## 6. How does it output parsed content?

LiteParse outputs in two formats, selected via `--format`:

### Text format (default: `--format text`)
Plain text with spatial layout preserved. Text comes back positioned as it appears on the page. This is the stdout default.

### JSON format (`--format json`)
Structured JSON containing per-page data with precise bounding boxes for every text item:
```typescript
// Each text item includes:
{
  text: string,
  x: number,      // position
  y: number,
  width: number,
  height: number
}
```

The library API returns a result object with:
- `result.text` -- full document text with layout preserved
- `result.pages` -- array of per-page data, each with `textItems` array
- `result.json?.pages` -- structured JSON with bounding box coordinates per text item

**LiteParse does NOT output markdown.** Its output is either plain text or structured JSON with spatial/bounding-box data. This is a notable difference from LlamaParse, which outputs clean markdown. If you need markdown output, you would need to post-process the text/JSON yourself or use LlamaParse instead.

Additionally, the `lit screenshot` command can generate **PNG or JPG page images** at configurable DPI, useful for feeding pages to LLMs as visual input.

---
name: Raven Tech Stack Decisions
description: Confirmed technology choices for the Raven knowledge-base platform
type: project
---

Confirmed tech stack decisions (as of 2026-03-27):

- **Frontend**: Vue.js + Tailwind Plus (user has subscription)
- **CMS**: Strapi (https://github.com/strapi/strapi) for managing orgs and users
- **Auth**: Keycloak with custom SPI "reavencloak" (built by user)
- **Document Parsing**: LlamaParse (LlamaIndex cloud API) for PDFs, images, docs
- **Vector Store**: ParadeDB + pgvector on PostgreSQL
- **Voice/WebRTC**: TBD (LiveKit/Pipecat candidates)
- **Web scraping**: TBD (LlamaParse doesn't do crawling — need separate tool)

**Why:** User wants a multi-tenant knowledge-base platform where organizations can ingest documents/websites and interact via chatbot, voice agent, or WebRTC.

**How to apply:** All design and implementation decisions should align with these choices. Don't suggest alternatives to confirmed tools unless there's a blocking issue.

lines: - **Frontend**: Vue.js + Tailwind Plus (user has subscription)
- **CMS**: Strapi (https://github.com/strapi/strapi) for managing orgs and users
- **Auth**: Keycloak with custom SPI "reavencloak" (built by user)
-- **Document Parsing**: LlamaParse (LlamaIndex cloud API) for PDFs, images, docs

+- **Document Parsing**: LiteParse (https://github.com/run-llama/liteparse) — open-source, Apache 2.0, runs locally, no cloud/LLM dependency. Supports PDF natively, Office docs via LibreOffice, images via ImageMagick+Tesseract OCR. Does NOT parse HTML/web pages.
- **Vector Store**: ParadeDB + pgvector on PostgreSQL
- **Voice/WebRTC**: TBD (LiveKit/Pipecat candidates)
- **Web scraping**: TBD (LlamaParse doesn't do crawling — need separate tool)

input.prompt:
You are helping brainstorm a platform called "Raven" — a multi-tenant knowledge-base platform. Here's the confirmed stack:

- Frontend: Vue.js + Tailwind Plus
- CMS: Strapi (headless CMS) for managing orgs/users
- Auth: Keycloak with custom SPI "reavencloak"
- Document Parsing: LiteParse (open-source, local, Apache 2.0, TypeScript/Node.js)
- Vector Store: PostgreSQL + pgvector (with ParadeDB for full-text search)
- Web scraping: Needs a separate tool (LiteParse can't do HTML)

The platform hierarchy: Application → Organization → Knowledge Base. Users can upload docs/images/PDFs and add website URLs. The system parses everything, creates vector embeddings, and offers 3 interaction modes: embeddable chatbot, voice agent, WebRTC/WhatsApp voice.

**Your task:** Draft 2-3 backend architecture approaches with trade-offs. Consider:
1. Monolith vs microservices vs modular monolith
2. Language choice — Python (best LLM/AI ecosystem) vs Node.js (matches LiteParse which is TypeScript) vs hybrid
3. How Strapi fits as the CMS layer vs a custom backend
4. Job queue architecture for async document processing (parsing, embedding, scraping)
5. API design (REST vs GraphQL vs tRPC)

For each approach, list: architecture style, language(s), key components, pros, cons, and your recommendation. Keep it concise — bullet points, not essays. Write the output to /Users/jobinlawrance/Project/raven/docs/research/backend-architecture-approaches.md

This is research/drafting only.

input.prompt:
You are helping brainstorm a platform called "Raven" — a multi-tenant knowledge-base platform. The platform offers 3 ways to interact with a knowledge base:

1. **Embeddable Chatbot** — a chat widget that can be embedded in any website, queries the knowledge base via LLM
2. **Voice Agent** — voice interface over the knowledge base using STT/TTS
3. **WebRTC/WhatsApp** — real-time voice via WebRTC, connectable to WhatsApp calls

Research findings:
- STT: faster-whisper, Deepgram Nova-3, NVIDIA Parakeet
- TTS: Piper (MIT, lightweight), Coqui XTTS (voice cloning), Cartesia Sonic (lowest latency API)
- VAD: Silero VAD (industry standard, MIT)
- WebRTC: LiveKit (open-source SFU) or Daily.co
- Voice frameworks: LiveKit Agents or Pipecat (most flexible)
- WhatsApp: Meta's WhatsApp Business Calling API (July 2025) supports WebRTC natively

**Your task:** Draft 2-3 approaches for the interaction layer:
1. How to build the embeddable chatbot (iframe vs web component vs JS SDK)
2. Voice agent architecture (which framework, STT/TTS choices, self-hosted vs API)
3. WebRTC integration and WhatsApp connectivity
4. How all three share the same RAG pipeline underneath
5. Latency considerations for real-time voice

For each approach, list: components, pros, cons, and recommendation. Write output to /Users/jobinlawrance/Project/raven/docs/research/interaction-layer-approaches.md

This is research/drafting only.

input.prompt:
Research and draft 2-3 approaches for the RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) pipeline for a knowledge-base platform called "Raven".

Context:
- Documents are parsed by LiteParse (outputs text/JSON with bounding boxes)
- Web content is scraped separately (likely markdown output)
- Embeddings stored in PostgreSQL + pgvector
- Full-text search via ParadeDB (BM25)
- Multi-tenant: each organization has its own knowledge base
- Three interaction modes query the same pipeline: chatbot, voice agent, WebRTC

**Your task:** Draft approaches covering:
1. **Chunking strategy**: fixed-size, semantic, recursive, document-structure-aware
2. **Embedding model choice**: OpenAI ada-002/text-embedding-3, Cohere embed-v3, open-source (sentence-transformers, nomic-embed, BGE)
3. **LLM for chat/knowledge graph**: OpenAI GPT-4o, Anthropic Claude, self-hosted (Llama 3, Mistral), or multi-provider
4. **Knowledge graph creation**: LlamaIndex PropertyGraphIndex, Neo4j, or skip KG and use pure vector+BM25 hybrid search
5. **Retrieval strategy**: pure vector, hybrid (vector + BM25 via RRF), with reranking (Cohere rerank, cross-encoder)
6. **Pipeline orchestration**: LlamaIndex, LangChain, custom, or Haystack

For each approach, list: components, pros, cons, and recommendation. Write output to /Users/jobinlawrance/Project/raven/docs/research/rag-pipeline-approaches.md

This is research/drafting only.
input.name: draft-rag-pipeline

content.text:
5 agents are now drafting approach options in the background for: backend architecture, data model, interaction layer, web scraping, and RAG pipeline. While they work, here are the remaining critical questions I need from you — answering these will let me synthesize everything into a design:

**1. Backend language preference?**
- a) **Python** — best AI/ML ecosystem (LlamaIndex, sentence-transformers, faster-whisper all native)
- b) **Node.js/TypeScript** — matches LiteParse, Strapi is Node-based
- c) **Hybrid** — Node.js for API/CMS layer, Python for AI/processing workers
- d) Something else (Go, Kotlin, etc.)?

**2. LLM provider for chat + embedding generation?**
- a) **OpenAI** (GPT-4o + text-embedding-3)
- b) **Anthropic Claude**
- c) **Multi-provider** (let users choose per org)
- d) **Self-hosted** (Llama 3, Mistral via Ollama/vLLM)

**3. The "Application" concept — help me understand the hierarchy:**
- Is an Application like a product/project (e.g., "MIT Voice Assistant") that contains one org? Or can one Application have multiple orgs? Who creates Applications vs. Organizations?

**4. Deployment target?**
- a) **Docker Compose** (self-hosted, simplest)
- b) **Kubernetes** (production-grade, scalable)
- c) **Cloud-managed** (AWS/GCP specific services)
- d) Start with Docker Compose, design for K8s migration later

**5. MVP scope — what ships first?**
- a) Full platform (all 3 interaction modes from day one)
- b) **Chatbot first**, then voice, then WebRTC
- c) Different priority order?

**6. Knowledge graph — do you actually need a graph database (Neo4j), or is hybrid vector+BM25 search sufficient?**
- a) Full knowledge graph (Neo4j/PropertyGraphIndex)
- b) Pure vector + BM25 hybrid search (simpler, pgvector + ParadeDB)
- c) Start with hybrid search, add KG later if needed

<result>Done. Here is the summary:

**Actions taken:**
- Created the directory `/Users/jobinlawrance/Project/raven/docs/research/`
- Drafted three backend architecture approaches with trade-off analysis

**File created:**
- `/Users/jobinlawrance/Project/raven/docs/research/backend-architecture-approaches.md` -- Full architecture comparison document

**Key findings / structure of the document:**

1. **Approach 1 -- Node.js Modular Monolith**: Single language (TypeScript), tRPC for type safety, BullMQ for jobs, LiteParse in-process. Simplest to deploy but weakest AI/ML ecosystem access.

2. **Approach 2 -- Hybrid Node.js API + Python AI Workers**: Node.js handles API/ingestion, Python handles AI/RAG/voice. Best flexibility and scaling but highest operational complexity.

3. **Approach 3 -- Python-First with Node.js Sidecar**: FastAPI core, Celery for jobs, thin Node.js sidecar just for LiteParse. Richest AI ecosystem but loses frontend type safety.

**Recommendation**: Start with Approach 1 (Node.js modular monolith) for MVP speed, evolve into Approach 2 by introducing a Python AI service only when you need local models or advanced RAG. This avoids premature complexity while keeping the Python door open. The document also calls out four decisions to lock in early: multi-tenant DB schema design, embedding model choice, web scraper selection, and API contract definition.</result>

- **Crawl4AI** is the best Apache 2.0 alternative with built-in chunking strategies that directly benefit embedding pipelines, though it is Python-only (Node.js access via Docker API).
- **Custom Playwright + Crawlee** offers maximum control with permissive licensing but requires 2-4 weeks of development.
- The primary decision driver is **license tolerance** (AGPL vs Apache 2.0) and **backend language** (Node.js vs Python).</result>

4. **Shared RAG**: Single gRPC streaming service (`RavenRAG.Query()`) called by all three surfaces, with tenant isolation and conversation history.
5. **Latency**: Target sub-1-second speech-to-speech via streaming at every stage, sentence-boundary TTS dispatch, and co-located infrastructure. Detailed latency budgets are included per pipeline stage.</result>

**Three approaches documented:**
1. **Approach A (Pragmatic Hybrid)** -- Recommended starting point. Custom pipeline, OpenAI embeddings, Claude LLM, hybrid vector+BM25 via RRF, Cohere reranking, no knowledge graph. Fastest to ship.
2. **Approach B (Self-Hosted & Cost-Optimized)** -- `nomic-embed-text` or BGE embeddings, Llama 3.1 70B, self-hosted cross-encoder reranker, Haystack orchestration. For high-volume or data-sovereignty scenarios.
3. **Approach C (Knowledge-Graph-Enhanced)** -- Adds Neo4j + LlamaIndex PropertyGraphIndex for multi-hop relational queries. Highest ceiling, highest complexity.

**Key recommendation:** Start with Approach A (4-6 week MVP), then layer in B or C components based on real usage patterns -- cost pressure favors B, relational query needs favor C.</result>

- Reviewed existing Raven research docs (`paradedb-pgvector-research.md`, `voice-agent-webrtc-research.md`) to align with established architectural decisions (Postgres + pgvector + ParadeDB, multi-tenant, voice agent via WebRTC).

**Three approaches documented:**
1. **Approach A (Pragmatic Hybrid)** -- Recommended starting point. Custom pipeline, OpenAI embeddings, Claude LLM, hybrid vector+BM25 via RRF, Cohere reranking, no knowledge graph. Fastest to ship.
2. **Approach B (Self-Hosted & Cost-Optimized)** -- `nomic-embed-text` or BGE embeddings, Llama 3.1 70B, self-hosted cross-encoder reranker, Haystack orchestration. For high-volume or data-sovereignty scenarios.
3. **Approach C (Knowledge-Graph-Enhanced)** -- Adds Neo4j + LlamaIndex PropertyGraphIndex for multi-hop relational queries. Highest ceiling, highest complexity.

**Key recommendation:** Start with Approach A (4-6 week MVP), then layer in B or C components based on real usage patterns -- cost pressure favors B, relational query needs favor C.</result>

<summary>Agent "Draft data model approaches" completed</summary>
<result>Here is a summary of what was done:

**Actions taken:**
- Reviewed existing research (`paradedb-pgvector-research.md`, `voice-agent-webrtc-research.md`) to align the data model with prior architectural decisions
- Drafted a comprehensive data model design document covering all six requested areas

**File created:**
- `/Users/jobinlawrance/Project/raven/docs/research/data-model-approaches.md`

**Key findings and decisions in the document:**

1. **Multi-tenancy:** Three approaches detailed with trade-off matrix. Approach A (shared schema + row-level `application_id` + PostgreSQL RLS) recommended as starting point due to Strapi compatibility, operational simplicity, and sufficient isolation for most SaaS use cases. Migration path to schema-per-tenant outlined if compliance demands evolve.

2. **Core entities:** 13 tables designed -- `applications`, `organizations`, `users`, `organization_members`, `knowledge_bases`, `documents`, `sources`, `chunks`, `embeddings`, `chat_sessions`, `chat_messages`, `voice_sessions`, `voice_turns`, plus `api_keys` and `processing_events`.

3. **Strapi mapping:** Clear split -- Strapi manages CRUD for config entities (Application, Organization, KnowledgeBase, Document metadata, Source); PostgreSQL directly manages high-throughput tables (chunks, embeddings, sessions, messages). Lifecycle hooks in Strapi trigger processing pipelines.

4. **pgvector design:** Chunks and embeddings are separate tables (supporting re-embedding and multiple models). HNSW index with cosine similarity. Hybrid search pattern using RRF fusion of pgvector semantic search + ParadeDB BM25 keyword search. Default: 512-token chunks, 50-token overlap, `text-embedding-3-small` at 1536 dimensions.

5. **Access control:** Four-layer defense-in-depth -- Keycloak authentication, API middleware authorization, organization-role-based access (owner/admin/member/viewer), and PostgreSQL RLS as the final safety net.

6. **Processing state machine:** Seven states (`queued` -> `parsing` -> `chunking` -> `embedding` -> `ready`, with `failed` and `reprocessing`), plus an audit `processing_events` table for observability.</result>

content:
For the backend language preference, I would go with Golang as the main backend language, with probably a framework like Gin or FastHTTP. It will be a hybrid one, wherein to deal with AI code and LLMs like Llama light passer, we will use Python as well, so the Golang code and the Python code can communicate with each other either via gRPC or by direct function calling from Golang to the Python code, whatever will be efficient and secure.
LLM provider for the chat will be multi-provider. We will let the user choose per organization. We will start only with the hosted ones, not the self-hosted ones, mainly focusing on Anthropic cloud, but again the user should have the option to select any of the providers that they want, along with the keys that they can get.
The application concept is just like a multi-tenant organization, so let's say MIT can be an organization as well, and inside that we will have multiple sub-organizations. I don't know how to name it; what is a proper way to do it? Basically, let's say if we have a company conglomerate like Alphabet, Google's Alphabet, and each team will be sub-organizations like Chrome, Android, and so forth. It's just an example, so let's name what will be appropriate here.
Deployment target will be Docker Compose, simple self-hosted, or along with that we will give a priced version for a cloud-managed one. They can either install it using our AWS plugins or script that we will create, or they can just use it via our hosted cloud. We will come up with the pricing strategy later so that at least we can break even to begin with before we start making profit.
MVP scope:
- voice one, the first one, chatbot
- web RTC
- later knowledge graph
I think we will start with a hybrid vector BM25 with PG vector and parade DB. We will add Neo4j or Quadrant later.

content.text:
All 5 research agents are done. Here's what's been drafted in `docs/research/`:

| File | Key Recommendation |
|---|---|
| `backend-architecture-approaches.md` | Start Node.js modular monolith, evolve to hybrid with Python AI workers |
| `data-model-approaches.md` | Shared schema + row-level security (RLS), 13 core tables, Strapi for CRUD, direct PG for high-throughput |
| `web-scraping-approaches.md` | Firecrawl (AGPL) fastest path, Crawl4AI (Apache 2.0) best permissive alternative |
| `rag-pipeline-approaches.md` | Pragmatic hybrid: OpenAI embeddings + Claude LLM + vector+BM25 via RRF, no knowledge graph initially |
| `interaction-layer-approaches.md` | Web Component chatbot, LiveKit Agents for voice, direct WhatsApp Business Calling API via WebRTC |

All approaches are drafted with 2-3 options each. I need your answers to the 6 questions I asked earlier to collapse these options into a single design. Here they are again briefly:

1. **Backend language?** Python / Node.js / Hybrid / Other?
2. **LLM provider?** OpenAI / Claude / Multi-provider / Self-hosted?
3. **Application vs Organization hierarchy** — who creates what?
4. **Deployment?** Docker Compose / K8s / Cloud / Start simple, design for scale?
5. **MVP scope?** All 3 interaction modes, or chatbot first?
6. **Knowledge graph?** Neo4j now / Hybrid search only / Add KG later?

input.old_string:
Confirmed tech stack decisions (as of 2026-03-27):

- **Frontend**: Vue.js + Tailwind Plus (user has subscription)
- **CMS**: Strapi (https://github.com/strapi/strapi) for managing orgs and users
- **Auth**: Keycloak with custom SPI "reavencloak" (built by user)
- **Document Parsing**: LiteParse (https://github.com/run-llama/liteparse) — open-source, Apache 2.0, runs locally, no cloud/LLM dependency. Supports PDF natively, Office docs via LibreOffice, images via ImageMagick+Tesseract OCR. Does NOT parse HTML/web pages.
- **Vector Store**: ParadeDB + pgvector on PostgreSQL
- **Voice/WebRTC**: TBD (LiveKit/Pipecat candidates)
- **Web scraping**: TBD (LlamaParse doesn't do crawling — need separate tool)

**Why:** User wants a multi-tenant knowledge-base platform where organizations can ingest documents/websites and interact via chatbot, voice agent, or WebRTC.

**How to apply:** All design and implementation decisions should align with these choices. Don't suggest alternatives to confirmed tools unless there's a blocking issue.
input.new_string:
Confirmed tech stack decisions (as of 2026-03-27):

- **Backend**: Go (Gin or FastHTTP) as main backend + Python for AI/LLM workers. Communicate via gRPC or direct function calls.
- **Frontend**: Vue.js + Tailwind Plus (user has subscription)
- **CMS**: Strapi (https://github.com/strapi/strapi) for managing orgs and users
- **Auth**: Keycloak with custom SPI "reavencloak" (built by user)
- **Document Parsing**: LiteParse (https://github.com/run-llama/liteparse) — open-source, Apache 2.0, local
- **Vector Store**: PostgreSQL + pgvector + ParadeDB (hybrid vector+BM25). Neo4j/Qdrant deferred.
- **LLM**: Multi-provider BYOK (user brings own keys per org). Anthropic Claude primary, but user selects provider.
- **Deployment**: Docker Compose (self-hosted) + future cloud-managed (priced, AWS scripts/plugins)
- **MVP order**: Chatbot first → Voice agent → WebRTC/WhatsApp
- **Hierarchy**: Organization → Sub-units (naming TBD) → Knowledge Bases

**Why:** User wants a multi-tenant knowledge-base platform where organizations can ingest documents/websites and interact via chatbot, voice agent, or WebRTC. BYOK model for LLM providers.

**How to apply:** All design and implementation decisions should align with these choices. Backend is Go-first, Python only for AI workloads. Don't suggest Node.js backend alternatives.

input.prompt:
Draft the **System Architecture** section of a design document for "Raven" — a multi-tenant knowledge-base platform. Use these CONFIRMED decisions:

**Stack:**
- Backend: Go (Gin or FastHTTP) — main API server
- Python: AI/LLM worker service — document parsing, embedding, RAG queries
- Go ↔ Python communication: gRPC (preferred for structured, typed, streaming RPC)
- Frontend: Vue.js + Tailwind Plus
- CMS: Strapi — manages content types, admin UI for CRUD
- Auth: Keycloak with custom SPI "reavencloak"
- DB: PostgreSQL + pgvector + ParadeDB
- Document parsing: LiteParse (TypeScript/Node — needs thin wrapper or CLI invocation from Python)
- Deployment: Docker Compose

**Write a concise architecture section covering:**
1. High-level component diagram (describe in text/ascii)
2. Service breakdown: Go API server, Python AI worker, Strapi CMS, Keycloak, PostgreSQL, Redis (job queue)
3. Request flow for key operations: user login, create org, upload document, chat query
4. How Go API server delegates to Python AI worker via gRPC
5. How LiteParse (Node.js) fits — likely called by the Python worker via subprocess/CLI
6. Job queue design for async document processing (Redis + worker pattern)

Output as markdown suitable for a design spec. Keep it concise — architecture diagrams as ASCII art, bullet points for flows. Write to stdout, not a file.

content.text:
5 design section agents launched in parallel:
1. **System Architecture** — Go API + Python AI worker + gRPC + service breakdown
2. **Data Model** — entity design, hierarchy naming, RLS, BYOK key storage
3. **Ingestion Pipeline** — LiteParse + web scraper + chunking + embedding flow
4. **Interaction Layer** — chatbot MVP, voice Phase 2, WebRTC Phase 3
5. **Deployment & Auth** — Docker Compose, Keycloak integration, future cloud

While they draft, one quick design question on the **hierarchy naming**. For your Alphabet → Google/Chrome/Android pattern, here are the common SaaS conventions:

| Pattern | Top Level | Sub-Unit | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Organization → Workspace** | Alphabet | Google, Chrome, Android | Slack, Notion |
| **Organization → Team** | Alphabet | Google, Chrome, Android | GitHub |
| **Organization → Project** | Alphabet | Google, Chrome, Android | GCP, Vercel |
| **Tenant → Organization** | Alphabet | Google, Chrome, Android | Enterprise SaaS |

My recommendation: **Organization → Workspace**. Reasoning:
- "Workspace" implies an isolated environment with its own knowledge bases, members, and settings — which is exactly what each sub-unit needs
- "Team" feels too people-focused (doesn't map well to "MIT → Physics Department has a knowledge base")
- "Project" feels too short-lived
- "Tenant → Organization" adds unnecessary nesting

So the hierarchy would be: **Organization** (Alphabet) → **Workspace** (Google) → **Knowledge Base** (Android Docs KB)

Does "Organization → Workspace" work for you, or do you prefer a different naming?

## Ingestion Pipeline

### Overview

The ingestion pipeline transforms raw documents and web pages into searchable, retrievable knowledge. Every input — whether a file upload or a URL — follows the same lifecycle: **Submit → Queue → Parse/Scrape → Chunk → Embed → Index → Ready**.

### Supported Input Types

| Category | Formats |
|----------|---------|
| Documents | PDF, DOCX, Markdown (.md) |
| Images | PNG, JPG, TIFF (OCR via LiteParse) |
| Web | Any public URL (rendered and scraped) |

### Pipeline Stages

```
┌──────────────┐    ┌───────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌───────┐    ┌───────┐    ┌───────┐    ┌───────┐
│ Upload / URL │───▶│ Queue │───▶│ Parse/Scrape │───▶│ Chunk │───▶│ Embed │───▶│ Index │───▶│ Ready │
└──────────────┘    └───────┘    └──────────────┘    └───────┘    └───────┘    └───────┘    └───────┘
     Go API          Redis         Python Worker      Python       Ext. API    pgvector +
                                                      Worker       (BYOK)      ParadeDB
```

### File Processing Path

1. **Go API** receives the multipart upload, validates file type and size, and writes the file to **object storage** (S3/MinIO). A metadata record is created in PostgreSQL with status `queued`.
2. The API enqueues an ingestion job onto the **Redis job queue** containing the file reference, org ID, and embedding provider config.
3. **Python AI worker** picks up the job via gRPC, downloads the file from object storage, and invokes **LiteParse** (Node.js CLI, Apache 2.0) as a subprocess: `liteparse <file> --output json`. LiteParse handles PDF text extraction, DOCX conversion, image OCR, and Markdown normalization.
4. The extracted text proceeds through chunking → embedding → indexing (see below).

### URL Processing Path

1. **Go API** receives the URL, validates it, and enqueues a scrape job onto **Redis**.
2. **Python AI worker** picks up the job and invokes the **web scraper** (see recommendation below) to fetch and render the page, extracting clean Markdown/text content.
3. The extracted content proceeds through the same chunking → embedding → indexing path as file-based inputs.

### Chunking Strategy

The pipeline uses **recursive text splitting**:

- **Target chunk size:** ~512 tokens
- **Overlap:** 50 tokens between consecutive chunks
- Split hierarchy: paragraph boundaries → sentence boundaries → word boundaries
- Metadata preserved per chunk: source document ID, chunk index, section heading (if available), character offsets

This balances retrieval precision (chunks small enough to be topically coherent) with context retention (overlap prevents information loss at boundaries).

### Multi-Provider Embeddings (BYOK)

Each organization configures its embedding provider at the org level:

| Setting | Example |
|---------|---------|
| `embedding_provider` | `openai`, `cohere`, `voyage`, etc. |
| `embedding_model` | `text-embedding-3-small` |
| `embedding_api_key` | Encrypted, org-scoped |
| `embedding_dimensions` | 1536 |

The Python worker reads the org's embedding config from PostgreSQL and dispatches to the appropriate provider client. A thin adapter interface normalizes the call:

```python
class EmbeddingProvider(Protocol):
    def embed(self, texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]: ...
```

**Constraint:** All documents within a single org must use the same model/dimensions, since pgvector columns are fixed-width. Changing providers requires re-indexing.

Chunks are embedded in batches (up to provider rate limits) to minimize API round-trips.

### Indexing

Each chunk is written to PostgreSQL with:

- **pgvector** — stores the embedding vector for similarity search
- **ParadeDB BM25** — indexes the raw chunk text for full-text keyword search

At query time, both indexes are queried and results are merged via **Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)**.

### Error Handling and Retry Strategy

| Failure | Behavior |
|---------|----------|
| Parse failure (corrupt file, unsupported format) | Mark document as `failed`, surface error to user. No retry. |
| Scrape failure (timeout, 4xx/5xx) | Retry up to **3 times** with exponential backoff (5s, 30s, 120s). Then mark `failed`. |
| Embedding API error (rate limit, 5xx) | Retry up to **5 times** with exponential backoff. Job remains in queue. |
| Embedding API auth error (invalid key) | Fail immediately, mark `failed`, notify user of bad API key. |
| Partial failure (some chunks embedded, then error) | Resume from last successful chunk on retry (checkpoint-based). Already-indexed chunks are not re-processed. |

All jobs carry a **maximum TTL of 30 minutes** in the queue. Jobs exceeding this are moved to a dead-letter queue for inspection.

Document status is exposed via the API: `queued → processing → ready | failed`.

### Web Scraper Recommendation: Firecrawl vs Crawl4AI

| Criterion | Firecrawl | Crawl4AI |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **License** | AGPL-3.0 | Apache 2.0 |
| **Deployment** | Self-hosted or cloud API | Self-hosted (Python library) |
| **JS rendering** | Yes (built-in) | Yes (Playwright-based) |
| **Output quality** | Excellent — clean Markdown, LLM-optimized | Good — Markdown output, improving rapidly |
| **Crawl modes** | Single page + site-wide crawl + sitemap | Single page + multi-page crawl |
| **Integration effort** | REST API or Python SDK | Native Python, direct function calls |
| **License risk** | AGPL requires open-sourcing derivative work if distributed; self-hosted SaaS use is debated | No license risk |

**Recommendation: Crawl4AI (Apache 2.0).**

Firecrawl produces marginally better output, but its AGPL license creates legal complexity for a multi-tenant SaaS platform. Crawl4AI is Apache 2.0, integrates natively into the Python worker (no sidecar service needed), and its output quality is sufficient for the chunking and embedding pipeline. If scrape quality proves insufficient for specific sites, a future iteration can add site-specific extraction rules or swap in Firecrawl's cloud API as an optional premium tier behind a clean interface boundary.</result>

```
┌──────────────┐    ┌───────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌───────┐    ┌───────┐    ┌───────┐    ┌───────┐
│ Upload / URL │───▶│ Queue │───▶│ Parse/Scrape │───▶│ Chunk │───▶│ Embed │───▶│ Index │───▶│ Ready │
└──────────────┘    └───────┘    └──────────────┘    └───────┘    └───────┘    └───────┘    └───────┘
     Go API          Redis         Python Worker      Python       Ext. API    pgvector +
                                                      Worker       (BYOK)      ParadeDB
```

### File Processing Path

1. **Go API** receives the multipart upload, validates file type and size, and writes the file to **object storage** (S3/MinIO). A metadata record is created in PostgreSQL with status `queued`.
2. The API enqueues an ingestion job onto the **Redis job queue** containing the file reference, org ID, and embedding provider config.
3. **Python AI worker** picks up the job via gRPC, downloads the file from object storage, and invokes **LiteParse** (Node.js CLI, Apache 2.0) as a subprocess: `liteparse <file> --output json`. LiteParse handles PDF text extraction, DOCX conversion, image OCR, and Markdown normalization.
4. The extracted text proceeds through chunking → embedding → indexing (see below).

### URL Processing Path

1. **Go API** receives the URL, validates it, and enqueues a scrape job onto **Redis**.
2. **Python AI worker** picks up the job and invokes the **web scraper** (see recommendation below) to fetch and render the page, extracting clean Markdown/text content.
3. The extracted content proceeds through the same chunking → embedding → indexing path as file-based inputs.

### Chunking Strategy

The pipeline uses **recursive text splitting**:

- **Target chunk size:** ~512 tokens
- **Overlap:** 50 tokens between consecutive chunks
- Split hierarchy: paragraph boundaries → sentence boundaries → word boundaries
- Metadata preserved per chunk: source document ID, chunk index, section heading (if available), character offsets

This balances retrieval precision (chunks small enough to be topically coherent) with context retention (overlap prevents information loss at boundaries).

### Multi-Provider Embeddings (BYOK)

Each organization configures its embedding provider at the org level:

| Setting | Example |
|---------|---------|
| `embedding_provider` | `openai`, `cohere`, `voyage`, etc. |
| `embedding_model` | `text-embedding-3-small` |
| `embedding_api_key` | Encrypted, org-scoped |
| `embedding_dimensions` | 1536 |

The Python worker reads the org's embedding config from PostgreSQL and dispatches to the appropriate provider client. A thin adapter interface normalizes the call:

```python
class EmbeddingProvider(Protocol):
    def embed(self, texts: list[str]) -> list[list[float]]: ...
```

**Constraint:** All documents within a single org must use the same model/dimensions, since pgvector columns are fixed-width. Changing providers requires re-indexing.

Chunks are embedded in batches (up to provider rate limits) to minimize API round-trips.

### Indexing

Each chunk is written to PostgreSQL with:

- **pgvector** — stores the embedding vector for similarity search
- **ParadeDB BM25** — indexes the raw chunk text for full-text keyword search

At query time, both indexes are queried and results are merged via **Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)**.

### Error Handling and Retry Strategy

| Failure | Behavior |
|---------|----------|
| Parse failure (corrupt file, unsupported format) | Mark document as `failed`, surface error to user. No retry. |
| Scrape failure (timeout, 4xx/5xx) | Retry up to **3 times** with exponential backoff (5s, 30s, 120s). Then mark `failed`. |
| Embedding API error (rate limit, 5xx) | Retry up to **5 times** with exponential backoff. Job remains in queue. |
| Embedding API auth error (invalid key) | Fail immediately, mark `failed`, notify user of bad API key. |
| Partial failure (some chunks embedded, then error) | Resume from last successful chunk on retry (checkpoint-based). Already-indexed chunks are not re-processed. |

All jobs carry a **maximum TTL of 30 minutes** in the queue. Jobs exceeding this are moved to a dead-letter queue for inspection.

Document status is exposed via the API: `queued → processing → ready | failed`.

### Web Scraper Recommendation: Firecrawl vs Crawl4AI

| Criterion | Firecrawl | Crawl4AI |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **License** | AGPL-3.0 | Apache 2.0 |
| **Deployment** | Self-hosted or cloud API | Self-hosted (Python library) |
| **JS rendering** | Yes (built-in) | Yes (Playwright-based) |
| **Output quality** | Excellent — clean Markdown, LLM-optimized | Good — Markdown output, improving rapidly |
| **Crawl modes** | Single page + site-wide crawl + sitemap | Single page + multi-page crawl |
| **Integration effort** | REST API or Python SDK | Native Python, direct function calls |
| **License risk** | AGPL requires open-sourcing derivative work if distributed; self-hosted SaaS use is debated | No license risk |

**Recommendation: Crawl4AI (Apache 2.0).**

Firecrawl produces marginally better output, but its AGPL license creates legal complexity for a multi-tenant SaaS platform. Crawl4AI is Apache 2.0, integrates natively into the Python worker (no sidecar service needed), and its output quality is sufficient for the chunking and embedding pipeline. If scrape quality proves insufficient for specific sites, a future iteration can add site-specific extraction rules or swap in Firecrawl's cloud API as an optional premium tier behind a clean interface boundary.</result>

# Interaction Layer — Raven Platform

## Phase 1: Embeddable Chatbot (MVP)

### 1.1 Architecture — Web Component

Raven ships a framework-agnostic **Web Component** (`<raven-chat>`) built as a single JS bundle. Site owners embed it with one script tag:

```html
<script src="https://cdn.raven.dev/chat.js"></script>
<raven-chat kb="kb_abc123" api-key="rk_live_..."></raven-chat>
```

The component uses Shadow DOM for style isolation, ensuring no CSS collisions with the host page. It renders a floating launcher button, expandable chat window, and message thread. Appearance (colors, logo, welcome message, position) is configured via attributes or the admin dashboard and fetched at init time from a lightweight `/v1/chatbot/{kb}/config` endpoint.

### 1.2 Authentication

- Each **knowledge base** has one or more **publishable API keys** (`rk_live_...`). These are domain-scoped — the Go API validates the key and checks the `Origin` / `Referer` header against an allow-list configured by the org admin.
- No end-user login is required. The API key identifies the tenant and knowledge base; rate limiting and usage metering are applied per key.
- Admin operations (dashboard, config changes) use a separate **secret key** authenticated via standard Bearer tokens over HTTPS.

### 1.3 Chat API — Streaming via SSE

The chat endpoint uses **Server-Sent Events (SSE)** over `POST /v1/chat/{kb}/completions`:

- SSE is chosen over WebSocket for the MVP because it is simpler to deploy behind CDNs/load balancers, works naturally with HTTP/2, and the interaction pattern is request-response (not bidirectional).
- The client sends a JSON body (`message`, `conversation_id`, optional `metadata`). The server responds with `Content-Type: text/event-stream`, streaming tokens as `data:` frames and a final `[DONE]` sentinel.
- Each event includes a `type` field: `token`, `source` (retrieved document references), `error`, or `done`.

### 1.4 RAG Query Flow

```
User message
  │
  ▼
Go API (auth, rate-limit, load conversation history)
  │  gRPC call
  ▼
Python AI Worker
  ├─ 1. Embed query  (provider-specific embedding model)
  ├─ 2. Hybrid search
  │     ├─ Vector similarity search  (pgvector / Qdrant)
  │     └─ BM25 keyword search       (Tantivy / Elasticsearch)
  ├─ 3. Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) to merge results
  ├─ 4. Rerank top-K (Cohere Rerank or cross-encoder)
  └─ 5. LLM generation (BYOK: Anthropic Claude default, org-selected provider)
         │  stream tokens via gRPC server-streaming
         ▼
Go API  ──► SSE stream ──► Client
```

The Go API opens a **gRPC server-streaming** call to the Python worker. Tokens are forwarded to the client as SSE events with minimal buffering. Source citations are sent as a discrete event before the `done` frame.

### 1.5 Conversation History Management

- Each conversation is identified by a `conversation_id` (UUID), returned on the first response and reused by the client on subsequent messages.
- The Go API stores messages in **PostgreSQL** (`conversations` and `messages` tables), scoped to the knowledge base.
- Before calling the AI worker, the Go API loads the last **N turns** (configurable, default 10) and passes them as context. A sliding window with token-budget awareness is used to stay within LLM context limits.
- Conversations have a configurable **TTL** (default 24 h for anonymous sessions). Admins can enable persistent history if they authenticate end-users via a JWT pass-through.

### 1.6 Admin Dashboard (Vue.js + Tailwind Plus)

The Vue.js SPA provides:

- **Chatbot Configurator** — live preview of widget appearance (theme colors, avatar, welcome text, suggested questions).
- **Test Sandbox** — embedded `<raven-chat>` instance pointed at a staging key so admins can test against their knowledge base before going live.
- **Analytics** — conversation volume, unanswered-question rate, top queries, source-hit frequency.
- **API Key Management** — create/revoke publishable keys, set domain allow-lists and rate limits.

---

## Phase 2: Voice Agent (Deferred)

- Built on the **LiveKit Agents** framework, running as a Python worker alongside the existing AI worker.
- **STT**: faster-whisper (self-hosted, low latency) or Deepgram (managed, higher accuracy) — configurable per org.
- **TTS**: Piper (open-source, on-prem) or Cartesia (low-latency streaming) — configurable per org.
- The voice agent transcribes speech, feeds the text through the **same RAG pipeline** (hybrid search → RRF → rerank → LLM), and streams the response back as synthesized audio.
- LiveKit handles participant management, room state, and media transport.

---

## Phase 3: WebRTC / WhatsApp (Deferred)

- **WhatsApp**: Integrate via the **WhatsApp Business Calling API**, which uses WebRTC natively. Inbound calls are routed to a LiveKit room where the Raven voice agent joins as a participant.
- **WebRTC (browser)**: Expose a LiveKit room token endpoint so web clients can join a voice session directly, enabling a "call the assistant" button in the chatbot widget.
- **Room bridging**: A lightweight Go service manages LiveKit room lifecycle — creating rooms on inbound call, attaching the AI agent participant, and tearing down on hangup.</result>
